data.title: Guggenheim Total Return Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • The Fund (Institutional Class) returned 1.70 percent in the second quarter, outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, by 0.49 percent.
  • The Fund’s yield advantage over the benchmark continues to be the largest contributor to outperformance.
  • The Fund’s active duration positioning, including its overweight to the front end of the yield curve and underweight position at the long end, enhanced relative performance, benefiting from the steepening of the curve during the quarter.
  • An overweight position in high yield corporates coupled with effective security selection drove positive relative performance as high yield spreads tightened over the quarter.

Strategy and Positioning

  • The Fund opportunistically increased credit exposure as spreads widened during the tariff- driven selloff, then tactically reduced most of this exposure as spreads tightened later in the quarter.
  • The Fund maintained a modest overweight duration stance, with a preference for intermediate tenors.
  • The Fund partially covered an underweight position at the long end as market concerns about foreign selling and potential buying strikes proved overstated and value returned to the segment.
  • As volatility declined near quarter end, the Fund continued to upgrade portfolio quality by selectively trimming high yield and BBB corporate exposure, particularly where spreads had retraced the most.
  • The Fund maintained a strategic allocation to Treasury inflation- protected securities (TIPS) as breakeven rates have remained subdued since 2023, despite persistent upside inflation risks stemming from post-COVID structural supply chain changes and recent trade policy developments.
  • Looking forward, credit positioning favors mortgage credit, including Agency and non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), reflecting improved relative value versus investment-grade corporate bonds and stable underlying fundamentals.

QTD Contributors/Detractors

  • Carry contributed 1.45 percent to absolute return and 0.2 percent to relative return.
  • Duration contributed 0.1 percent on an absolute basis and 0.15 percent on a relative basis.
  • Spread tightening in high-yield corporates contributed on an absolute and relative basis.

Economic and Market Review

  • The economic landscape continues to be shaped by heightened policy volatility, with historic swings in tariff policy the main source of uncertainty. Despite declining from its peak, the effective tariff rate remains at its highest level in decades at about 15 percent, acting as a tax on the economy.
  • While the full impact of tariff hikes has yet to materialize, the coming months are expected to bring slow growth and rising inflation as businesses absorb some of the higher tariff costs through lower profit margins and pass the rest on to consumers.
  • While peak uncertainty is likely behind us, there is likely to be continued caution from businesses and consumers. Downside risks may be tempered by the administration’s reluctance to maintain policies that could inflict significant economic harm.
  • Meanwhile, the federal budget, shaped by the One Big Beautiful Bill, should provide a modest boost to growth, primarily in 2026. We expect both consumer and business spending should get a lift from tax cuts early next year.
  • Although the bill contains more cost cutting measures than had been anticipated, concerns remain about fiscal sustainability. Even with a potential boost from tariff revenues, fiscal deficits would exceed 6 percent of gross domestic product (GDP) over the next decade. We see U.S. real GDP growth of slightly below 1 percent in 2025, with a modest recovery in 2026.
  • In the near term, households are likely to experience reduced spending power due to higher prices on imported goods. Indeed, discretionary consumer spending has already seen a sharp pullback in the first half of the year. Business investment is also likely to slow, though AI-related investment remains a notable exception. Weaker consumer and business spending will likely lead to more labor market softening, with unemployment rising to around 4.5 percent by year end.
  • We see inflation rising above 3 percent by the fourth quarter, driven by tariff-related price increases. However, this is expected to be a one-time boost rather than a persistent trend. Underlying disinflationary forces—such as cooling housing and wage inflation—should eventually prevail as higher unemployment and reduced consumer spending power help contain broader price pressures.
  • The Fed’s cautious stance reflects a wait-and-see approach to evolving policy and economic conditions. The Fed may have already been lowering the policy rate again had the uncertainty from tariff policy not been introduced.
  • As some time has now passed, we expect easing to commence later this year as policy certainty increases and labor market weakness becomes more pronounced, with rate cuts accelerating in 2026 to 3.125 percent as the Fed gains comfort that tariff driven price increases will not be an ongoing source of inflation. The Fed is expected to view risks of labor market weakness as a more persistent problem than tariff driven inflation.

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

  1. Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class.
  2. The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2026, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund.
  3. The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales.
  4. Benchmark-Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 6.30.2025. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses.

Index data source: FundStation.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors. § In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities. § Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk. CLOs bear similar risks to investing in loans directly. § Investments in loans involve special types of risks, including credit, interest rate, counterparty, prepayment, liquidity, and valuation risks. Loans are often below investment grade, may be unrated, and typically offer a fixed or floating interest rate. § High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility. § The fund’s use of leverage, through borrowings or instruments such as derivatives, may cause the fund to be more volatile and riskier than if it had not been leveraged. The more a fund invests in leveraged instruments, the more the leverage will magnify any gains or losses on those investments. § Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to many of the same risks as leveraged instruments, such as derivatives. § You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. § Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the investment grade, U.S . dollar- denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS (agency and non-agency). The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index measures the market for USD- denominated non-investment grade, fixed rate, taxable corporate bonds. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch, and S&P is Bal/BB+/ BB+ or below. The index excludes emerging market debt.

This material is not intended as a recommendation or as investment advice of any kind, including in connection with rollovers, transfers, and distributions. Such material is not provided in a fiduciary capacity, may not be relied upon for or in connection with the making of investment decisions, and does not constitute a solicitation of an offer to buy or sell securities. All content has been provided for informational or educational purposes only and is not intended to be and should not be construed as legal or tax advice and/or a legal opinion. Always consult a financial, tax, and/or legal professional regarding your specific situation.

Read the fund’s prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) carefully before investing. It contains the fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information, which should be considered carefully before investing. Obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) at Guggenheim Investments.

data.title: Guggenheim Macro Opportunities Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • The Fund (Institutional Class) returned 2.27 percent in the second quarter, outperforming its benchmark, the ICE Bank of America U.S. 3-Month Treasury Bill Index, by 1.23 percent.
  • Positive performance was driven primarily by carry (earned income).
  • Spread tightening, following developments on trade policy, had a notable impact on performance over the quarter.
  • Duration contributed positively to return as intermediate tenors outperformed.

Strategy and Positioning

  • The Fund opportunistically increased credit exposure as spreads widened during the tariff-driven selloff, then tactically reduced most of this exposure as spreads tightened later in the quarter.
  • Capitalizing on reduced volatility near quarter end, the Fund selectively trimmed exposure to high yield sectors where spread retracement was most significant.
  • The Fund increased eurozone credit, reflecting improved relative value in non-U.S. sectors.
  • The Fund modestly extended duration, taking the opportunity to lock in higher yields.
  • The Fund added to non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) sector as the relative valuation has improved versus corporates and the sector benefits from stable underlying fundamentals.

QTD Contributors/Detractors

  • Carry contributed 1.5 percent to return, while duration contributed 0.3 percent to return.
  • Spread tightening across corporate credit, particularly high yield bonds, contributed to performance.
  • Spread widening in collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) and asset-backed securities (ABS) was a marginal detractor.

Economic and Market Review

  • The economic landscape continues to be shaped by heightened policy volatility, with historic swings in tariff policy the main source of uncertainty. Despite declining from its peak, the effective tariff rate remains at its highest level in decades at about 15 percent, acting as a tax on the economy.
  • While the full impact of tariff hikes has yet to materialize, the coming months are expected to bring slow growth and rising inflation as businesses absorb some of the higher tariff costs through lower profit margins and pass the rest on to consumers.
  • While peak uncertainty is likely behind us, there is likely to be continued caution from businesses and consumers. Downside risks may be tempered by the administration's reluctance to maintain policies that could inflict significant economic harm.
  • Meanwhile, the federal budget, shaped by the One Big Beautiful Bill, should provide a modest boost to growth, primarily in 2026. We expect both consumer and business spending should get a lift from tax cuts early next year.
  • Although the bill contains more cost-cutting measures than had been anticipated, concerns remain about fiscal sustainability. Even with a potential boost from tariff revenues, fiscal deficits would exceed 6 percent of gross domestic product (GDP) over the next decade. We see U.S. real GDP growth of slightly below 1 percent in 2025, with a modest recovery in 2026.
  • In the near term, households are likely to experience reduced spending power due to higher prices on imported goods. Indeed, discretionary consumer spending has already seen a sharp pullback in the first half of the year. Business investment is also likely to slow, though Al-related investment remains a notable exception. Weaker consumer and business spending will likely lead to more labor market softening, with unemployment rising to around 4.5 percent by year end.
  • We see inflation rising above 3 percent by the fourth quarter, driven by tariff-related price increases. However, this is expected to be a one-time boost rather than a persistent trend. Underlying disinflationary forces-such as cooling housing and wage inflation-should eventually prevail as higher unemployment and reduced consumer spending power help contain broader price pressures.
  • The Fed's cautious stance reflects a wait-and-see approach to evolving policy and economic conditions. The Fed may have already been lowering the policy rate again had the uncertainty from tariff policy not been introduced.
  • As some time has now passed, we expect easing to commence later this year as policy certainty increases and labor market weakness becomes more pronounced, with rate cuts as the Fed gains comfort that tariff driven price increases will not be an ongoing source of inflation. The Fed is expected to view risks of labor market weakness as a more persistent problem than tariff driven inflation.

Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments. Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to the many of the same risks as investments in derivatives. The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle. The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political, or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks). Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks. A highly liquid secondary market may not exist for the commodity-linked structured notes the fund invests in, and there can be no assurance that a highly liquid secondary market will develop. The fund’s exposure to the commodity markets may subject the fund to greater volatility as commodity-linked investments may be affected by changes in overall market movements, commodity index volatility, changes in interest rates, or factors affecting a particular industry or commodity. The fund’s investments in municipal securities can be affected by events that affect the municipal bond market. The fund’s investments in real estate securities subject the fund to the same risks as direct investments in real estate, which is particularly sensitive to economic downturns. The fund’s investments in restricted securities may involve financial and liquidity risk. You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government. Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

data.title: Nvidia: Bulls Ignore Valuation, Bears Ignore China
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) is defying the already high expectations placed on it. The company continues to post torrid growth even as revenue scales to the tens of billions per quarter. This comes even as the company has not yet made use of its Chinese market opportunity, which management estimates to be more than significant. As generative AI use cases expand and evolve, management expects demand to only increase moving forward.

I am far less bearish than many peers, as I expect both growth and valuations to keep up for the company. That said, the path to market-beating upside requires things to go a bit more perfectly than I’d prefer, thus justifying my neutral rating.

NVDA Stock Price

I last covered NVDA in June, where I downgraded the stock on account of aggressive valuations. The stock has risen 8% since then.

Even as the company continues to defy gravity with stunning result after stunning result, I am steadfast in my view that the math always works out in the end, to the potential detriment of shareholders at these prices.

NVDA Stock Key Metrics

NVDA is a mega-cap tech giant positioned as a true “bricks and shovels” supplier of the generative AI revolution. The company has what appears to be an insurmountable lead in GPUs, which, in comparison with CPUs, involves using software in order to accelerate performance. NVDA’s CUDA developer ecosystem was already thriving prior to the rise of generative AI and has only gotten stronger since. I note that to anyone asking if NVDA is a bubble, my response is that the business model is very much “legit” as there is no generative AI future without NVDA (though one could quibble on the valuation).

In the most recent quarter, NVDA generated $46.7 billion in revenue, up 6% from the previous quarter and up 56% from a year ago, exceeding guidance of $45 billion.

Nowadays, it is fair to just focus on the data center business, which saw revenues jump 56% YoY to $41.1 billion. The strong results could have been guessed well ahead of the release, given all the CapEx guidance given by the cloud titans and Meta Platforms (META).

The company ended the quarter with $56.8 billion of cash versus $8.5 billion of debt, representing a bulletproof net cash balance sheet. The company has been primarily splitting free cash flow towards share repurchases and growing the cash pile.

Management guided the third quarter to see 53.8% YoY revenue growth to $54 billion. That represents minimal sequential deceleration from the 56% rate in this past quarter and 17% QoQ growth. This was notably higher than the 16.7% QoQ growth rate posted in the third quarter of the last fiscal year. The company’s ability to sustain aggressive growth rates even at a large scale is unprecedented and truly surprising.

On the conference call, management noted that the guidance does not factor in any H20 sales to China, which they estimate might be in the “range of about $2 billion to $5 billion” if allowed in the quarter. Management also noted that they view the Chinese market to be “about $50 billion of opportunity” for this year, with expectations for “50% per year” growth. China appears to be an important bullish catalyst for the stock, one that can help the company sustain aggressive growth rates even as gravity is waiting on the doorstep. Management noted that outside of China, they still expect growth to continue due to several key factors, those being “reasoning agentic AI requiring orders of magnitude more training and inference compute, global build-outs for sovereign AI, enterprise AI adoption, and the arrival of physical AI and robotics.”

The general idea is that generative AI has unleashed the limits of what is possible, and the newer ideas will require even more NVDA GPUs than in the past. While I remain optimistic that NVDA will remain a crucial part in enabling generative AI and beyond, I find such verbiage to be reminiscent of what I saw in the 2021 tech stock bubble when many high-flying tech names had predicted aggressive growth that could continue over the long term—Twilio (TWLO) stands out as one of the names I remember guiding for 30% growth over the following handful of years (the reality would see growth slow to below double digits).

Is NVDA Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, NVDA traded hands at around 20x sales. Consensus estimates call for growth to decelerate meaningfully over the next few years.

The stock traded at a rich 38x earnings, but earnings are expected to grow faster than revenues due to operating leverage.

I have seen many investors go as far as to say that NVDA is a bubble, but I disagree there. While I do find the stock to be aggressively valued, the valuation is not quite aggressive enough to reach bubble status. In particular, I am doubtful that demand for its products will deteriorate as some bears might stipulate, but expect it to instead continue to grow (albeit at a slower pace). Generative AI has been around for several years now, but I expect it to have a similar growth trajectory as the internet, one that shows resilient and at times sudden growth due to the continued discovery of new use cases. NVDA appears to have an insurmountable lead ahead of the pack, and this may allow it to benefit from both market growth and incredible profit margins.

This kind of setup reminds me of Apple (AAPL), which I view as an important comparable. AAPL is several years beyond its fast-growth days but continues to command a rich valuation multiple due to the perception of a highly recurring business supported by a loyal ecosystem. I believe that NVDA is following in the same footsteps, and for that reason, I do not expect the multiple to collapse, for example, to 15x earnings once growth slows. Instead, I expect NVDA to command a 30x earnings multiple even as top-line growth slows to the single digits.

That is enough to suggest that the stock likely goes up rather than sideways or down moving forward. But is it enough to justify a buy rating? I do not think so. If NVDA traded at 30x earnings by 2029, then the stock is priced for around 11.4% annual returns. Sure, I think that is likely to beat the broader market returns, but I’d feel more comfortable if we used assumptions that priced in more of a margin of safety. If we instead assume that NVDA traded at 24x earnings by 2029, then the stock would be priced for around 5.3% annual returns over the next 4 years.

This exercise shows that in order for NVDA to outperform the market (assuming 7% to 8% returns for the broader market), the stock would need to command that premium earnings multiple over the long term.

NVDA Stock Conclusion

NVDA may have a significant catalyst in the near term in the form of upside from Chinese revenues. That might excite bullish investors who have undoubtedly been proven correct throughout the company’s growth story. I disregard any notion that NVDA and its business are in a bubble, but the valuation is undeniably rich, and that raises the stakes in the search for stock market alpha. I recommend investors instead search for bargains among other high-quality names, as I have done in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my neutral rating for the stock.

data.title: Nextracker: Valuation And The End Of Political Drama Suggest Taking Profits
data.description:
Fundamentally, Nextracker (NASDAQ:NXT) stock looks ridiculously cheap. Even with a 65% rally year-to-date, shares trade at a little over 16x guidance for fiscal 2026 (ending March) adjusted earnings per share. Given clear leadership in solar trackers, impressive growth (adjusted EPS rose 38% last year, though there's a bit of a politically driven pause on that front this year), and solar's long-term outlook, that multiple looks almost impossibly low.

That's because the multiple is somewhat deflated: The Section 45X tax credit is boosting profit margins currently, though that benefit will start to fade in a few years. Adjusted for 45X, the multiples assigned to Nextracker stock look much more reasonable – and perhaps high enough to incorporate even impressive growth through the end of the decade. Given relief around the industry and the impact of the "One Big Beautiful Bill" signed in July, there is a case here that NXT at this point is reasonably priced.

Indeed, the consensus sell-side estimate now projects just 7% appreciation over the next 12 months. And it's not terribly difficult to see why. This is an excellent business – one perhaps worth owning longer-term – but valuation simply is much more aggressive than a first glance suggests. At this point, there do seem to be better options elsewhere in the space, and at the very least, real questions about near-term catalysts for Nextracker stock.

The Effects Of 45X On Valuation

Updated guidance given with last month's fiscal first quarter release suggests NXT stock is exceptionally cheap. At the midpoint, shares trade at 16.2x adjusted earnings per share and about 12.5x on an EV/EBITDA basis.

But, again, those multiples incorporate the impact of the Section 45X tax credit, implemented under the Biden Administration to subsidize domestic production of goods related to clean energy. The effect on Nextracker profits is substantial. CFO Chuck Boynton said after Q1 that 45X proceeds would total about 9% to 10% of revenue this year.

That's a ~$360 million reduction to the cost of sales across the year, or close to half of the ~23%-24% projected adjusted EBITDA margins. Based on post-Q4 guidance for an effective tax rate in the 20%-25% range, that suggests 45X is benefiting this year's adjusted earnings per share by about $1.85.

Accounting for the tax credit, multiples here look very different. Adjusted EPS goes from $4.12 (the midpoint of post-Q1 guidance) to about $2.27, putting P/E just shy of 30x. EBITDA declines from ~$780 million to ~$420 million, which (accounting for Nextracker's tax receivable agreement) means EV/EBITDA jumps to about 23x. Peer comparisons here aren't particularly useful – NXT trades at a premium to rivals Array Technologies (ARRY) and FTC Solar (FTCI), but it should do so as it's a much better business – but the 45X-adjusted multiples on their face show that relatively strong growth remains priced in.

But the credit also inflates Nextracker's reported growth. The big jump in FY25 adjusted EPS, in particular, was driven in part by the fact that Nextracker excluded 45X from FY24 adjusted results while including the effect of the credit the following year. (This was largely due to accounting effects: In Q4 FY24, Nextracker recognized the cumulative impact of the credit as a reduction to cost of sales before switching to quarterly reductions starting in FY25.)

To be fair, these calculations are relatively blunt and perhaps overstate the practical effect of the tax credit. Suppliers and customers know the credit exists, which of course affects negotiating power and pricing. It's too aggressive to argue that in a world without 45X, Nextracker's operating margins would be 10 percentage points lower. And the value of the credits through 2030 (which is not included in these multiples) is not immaterial. On a net present value basis, it's likely close to $2 billion, or nearly 20% of the current market cap.

Still, the broad point does hold. Accounting for that credit, Nextracker is actually a business trading at a mid-20s price-to-free-cash-flow multiple while posting high-single-digit/low-double-digit growth. In other words, the underlying business here at this point looks relatively reasonably priced.

The YTD Rally In NXT Stock

And that credit is going away. As originally implemented, 45X would be offered at a 25% lower rate in 2030-2032 before ending completely in 2033. Uncertainty about the credit under the Trump Administration has created immense volatility in NXT and other solar plays, but (seemingly) final resolution has heartened the market and led the sector sharply higher since April. (The Invesco Solar ETF (TAN) is up 60% just since April 8.)

Guidance from the Internal Revenue Service around 45X seems to limit the threat created by an executive order issued three days after the OBBB was signed. That EO directed new guidance that would tighten "beginning of construction rules" that determine eligibility for 45X and other, smaller, credits. But the IRS last month issued a notice that allows for a 5% "safe harbor" (i.e., if 5% of the projected cost is spent, construction has begun) through September and an actual physical work test that must be satisfied by next July. From that point, developers have four years, meaning that in practice 45X should last through mid-2030.

That's not that much different from the pre-Trump expectation. Clearly, for the industry, the worst-case scenario has been averted. And the market has responded in kind. NXT saw a two-day, 25% rally; ARRY jumped even higher. Guggenheim upgraded Nextracker stock, calling the company a key beneficiary of the IRS notice.

But it's worth noting that even Guggenheim only took its target to $74. And with the tax credit drama seemingly resolved (though of course that is always subject to change in the current environment), there is the question about what catalysts remain. Obviously, solar plays remain potential beneficiaries of the generative artificial intelligence trade (which is still going reasonably strong). There may be other, stronger AI plays, however, and in solar I prefer First Solar (FSLR), which I continue to own.

The case for NXT I laid out in March was that shares were worth owning (as I did at the time) in large part because the risks to 45X seemed overblown. That case has played out. I'm not convinced there is a strong enough case left, particularly near an all-time high.

The Case For Nextracker Stock

That's not to say that there is no case left for owning NXT. I may well regret my decision to exit here. Solar should be an AI beneficiary. As executives from Nextracker (and other industry players) have argued, even excluding credits, solar is getting close to parity on costs, particularly as storage technology improves; other fuels for electricity (obviously nuclear, and often even natural gas) are not likely to be up fast enough to meet AI demand.

Valuation is more aggressive than it looks but not that aggressive; treating net present value of 45X credits as cash in a model, P/E and P/FCF are in the low 20s. For investors who believe in the industry long-term, those multiples seem at worst fair.

And this is a good business. We have about 15 years of market history now that strongly suggests investors should own one of these three stocks:

In attractive end markets, winners have kept winning. Nextracker is still taking share: It's been No. 1 in the space for a decade, and in 2024 went from 23% to 26% in market share, per data cited in the Q1 shareholder letter.

The expansion beyond tracking is intriguing as well. Nextracker has spent about $150 million on a series of acquisitions to expand its reach and its revenue base. Bentek manufactures eBOS (electrical balance of system) products, which can be packaged with Nextracker's legacy trackers. OnSight provides autonomous robots for inspecting solar farms. SenseHawk creates 3-D maps of solar sites using drone imagery, and Amir Robotics manufactures robots that clean the actual panels.

Given there are some long-term concerns about tracker growth (what happens when newfield development starts decelerating?), these moves not only expand the addressable market but also offer more consistent recurring revenue as well. Nextracker said after Q4 it thought it could get one-third of its revenue outside of trackers within five years, which alone suggests mid-single-digit consolidated growth (that figure is not starting at zero). And given that the successful software offering – clearly a key differentiator against competitors – itself came from an acquisition, there should be some confidence that Nextracker can build up these newly acquired businesses within its walls.

Personally, I'm sympathetic to that case. The broad risk to exiting here is, in this bull market, exiting good businesses solely because of valuation usually has been a bad decision. In the case of NXT, it may be so again.

But after the volatility of the past year-plus, there is the possibility that NXT may offer another, lower, price at some point in the not too distant future. As I wrote in March, NXT wasn't an easy "Buy" then, and it's not an easy "Sell" now. For me, at least, it's a "Sell" nonetheless.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VDC: Vanguard Tackles The Popular, Low-Volatility Consumer Staples Sector Via This ETF
data.description:
The Consumer Staples sector, also known as consumer non-cyclical or simply as consumer defensive stocks, is one of 11 primary sectors of the S&P 500 index. That classification extends to smaller stocks as well as those outside of the US market.

What is a consumer staple?

Everyday items that consumers tend to purchase regularly and with less regard to cost than, say, cars, TVs, and leisure travel, are referred to on Wall Street as staples. They are the things we need, not the things we want. Examples include packaged food and drinks, dish detergent, soap, and toothpaste. That said, within staples, there is also a hierarchy, particularly when it comes to price.

We experience this every time we go to the grocery store. You can buy the name brand because you like it so much and are loyal to it. Or, you can decide that price is more important and buy a store-produced brand.

The first ETF devoted to this sector was The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLP), which debuted for market trading back in 1998, five years after the first ETF launched. It did so along with ETFs for the other S&P 500 sectors.

The Vanguard Consumer Staples Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VDC) started trading in early 2004, at the same time that the iconic investment manager also came out with a mutual fund version of VDC. That's one of the first things investors should understand about this and many other funds that existed in mutual fund form prior to or in sync with the ETF "clone" version of that fund.

While it is not assured that the two funds will perform the same way, in the case of Vanguard, which staked its claim in the fund management business last century with the goal of delivering index-tracking funds to investors at a low cost, we can see there's virtually no difference. We also see that over the first 20+ years of VDC's tenure, the ultimate returns for investors were enormous.

However, investors have different objectives, and the stock market is not always as buoyant as it was during this period. So, in exploring VDC as an investment, investors should do the work to understand what this is and isn't. This sector of the S&P 500 has some distinct features that tend to help it in some market climates and hold it back in others.

VDC: covers a historically "defensive" sector

In the modern world of AI, algorithmic trading, and nearly every significant market segment accessible through an ETF (or more than one ETF, in the case of consumer staples), some market areas get stuck with their reputations, for better or for worse.

In the case of VDC and the consumer staples sector, no one will confuse it with hot tech companies. Similar to retail in this respect, there might be a hot product that comes along, but it doesn't have the sustained lift to the stock prices here as it might for the components of a high-tech ETF.

Staples tend to trade for lower valuations than higher-growth companies. Their growth tends to be more stable and predictable. That makes them a sought-after sector for investors during recessions, relative to the rest of the market.

It also tends to put this sector, and thus ETFs like VDC, in the spotlight when markets experience sudden turmoil. The common expression is something like this: "You still need to eat, brush your teeth, and use the bathroom." So the necessities rise in priority. This was perhaps no more on display than back in 2020 when the Covid-19 pandemic erupted. Staples flew off the shelves. Cars and airline tickets? Not so much.

Low relative volatility: VDC's blessing and curse

In fact, when we smooth out returns from the first 20 years of VDC's existence, it turns out that on a 5-year rolling basis, it spent most of that time trailing the S&P 500, sometimes by wide margins. However, in the period that captures the global financial crisis, a sustained period of economic weakness and uncertainty for consumers (far-left side of the chart), it turned the tables on the broader index, consistently outperforming until the proverbial coast was clear.

Another way to analyze this same lengthy period of history for VDC is to pair it against its cousin ETF that covers the tech sector. Below, we see a consistent pattern using quarterly total returns. Virtually none of them resulted in VDC outperforming tech on the upside. But in down markets for tech, VDC nearly always held its ground better, even if it too lost money. That is partly due to the defensive nature of the sector and partly owing to the reputation it has built over the decades.

The Price-Earnings (P/E) ratio for the staples sector tends to be lower than that of the S&P 500, since the stocks grow more slowly. So investors are less apt to pay up in valuation to own them. However, in markets that get frothy, such as what we saw during the dot-com bubble (far-left side of the charts), staples can trade at gaudy multiples for years at a time.

However, this typically leads to a fallout later on. The difficult part for VDC holders is determining when.

VDC's holdings: the brands you know (and maybe trust)

The table below is a snapshot in time (7/31/2025), but it does show that, like most other S&P 500 sectors, staples and VDC tend to be top-heavy. This is presented primarily to offer you a sense of the types of mostly familiar firms that occupy VDC. More than most sectors, the brand name stocks here tend to provide investors with a sense of familiarity, especially if they are new to investing. Most novice investors have heard of several of these firms, be they retailers, candy icons, soda giants, or makers of pickles and ketchup.

That brings up another feature of VDC and the staples sector. More than most, it tends to be a market area where strategic combinations occur. The merger of Kraft and Heinz in 2015, orchestrated by none other than legendary investor Warren Buffett, was one such event. And the subsequent breakup of that company into two stocks once again, about a decade later, is also par for the course with staples.

As with any sector, industry, or theme, time changes what is favored and not by staple investors. Since VDC is an index ETF, its holdings will change over time. Here's an example of that, looking at the top-weighted stocks in VDC, but nearly 10 years apart. This is all based on the market cap of the stocks in comparison to each other. So mergers and other corporate events do play a role.

While the US stock market over time has become a lower-yield market, staples stocks, and thus VDC, tend to be one of the higher-yielding sectors within the S&P 500. The reason is simple: when your business is a slow but steady grower and cash flow generation is predictable, management can reliably pay a dividend without worrying about having to cease doing so. That is evident in this piece of VDC history below.

VDC is a Vanguard ETF, and that means low cost. It is what put the company on the map and made it essentially the pioneer in what are now ETFs. Vanguard was a fairly late and reluctant entrant in the ETF business. However, there came a point where investor preference for buying and selling their index funds on the stock exchange and intraday instead of at the market closing price (as mutual funds do) made it impossible to ignore.

So, the skinny expense ratio, as well as the giant size, of VDC (as of 9/3/25), are an indication that Vanguard's sector ETFs have completed the transition to market leadership in ETFs as well.

Who should consider investing in the Vanguard Consumer Staples Index Fund ETF?

Now that we know the territory here, the key decision for any investor in a sector ETF is to determine if it fits in their overall portfolio. And if so, how? The lower correlation and lower volatility of VDC likely deliver versus an S&P 500 ETF might attract some investors. Because you know you are less likely to get a "closet S&P 500 index fund" here.

The offsetting feature is the tendency for VDC to lag in strong bull markets, especially those where investors are fixated on tech stocks. Ultimately, it is a matter of doing your homework, recognizing that all stock investing carries risk, and truly connecting with what your objectives are and what is in the ETF packages you own.

This article answers these three main questions about VDC:

  • Is VDC less volatile than the overall market?
  • What type of investor is VDC best suited for?
  • Does VDC tend to perform better than other sectors during bear markets and recessions?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Reverses
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 1.9 percentage points to 32.7%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the sixth time in 10 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 2.0 percentage points to 23.9%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 59th time in 61 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 3.9 percentage points to 43.4%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 40th time in 42 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 5.8 percentage points to –10.7%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 29th time in 31 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members how they would describe the current state of the economy.

Here is how they responded:

  • Great: 4.0%
  • Good: 34.9%
  • Mixed: 54.7%
  • Lousy: 5.7%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 0.7%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 32.7%, down 1.9 percentage points
  • Neutral: 23.9%, down 2.0 percentage points
  • Bearish: 43.4%, up 3.9 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: Still A Buy Again After Q3 Earnings
data.description:
Since reporting second-quarter results in June 2025, Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (NYSE:HPE) gained nearly 23%, beating the S&P 500’s 8.37% return. On September 3, the firm posted third-quarter results that exceeded revenue and earnings expectations. HPE stock rose by as much as around 5% before settling around 3% higher.

HPE earns a reiterated Buy rating for the solid growth and for the fourth quarter and full-year outlook.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise's Q3 Results

HPE reported non-GAAP earnings per share of $0.44 after revenue grew by 18.5% Y/Y to $9.14 billion. The firm highlighted non-GAAP gross margins falling by 1.9 pts to 29.9%. GAAP EPS also fell slightly Y/Y. However, cash flow generated from operations was $1.3 billion, while cash flow was $790 million. This enabled the firm to declare a $0.13 quarterly dividend for a 2.2% forward yield. Income investors may not find the yield appealing, but the ~23% total return since June annualizes to a nearly 100% return.

Looking ahead, the Seeking Alpha Quant grades on HPE stock suggest modest upside from here. The valuation grade fell to a B+ as the share price neared its 52-week high of $24.66.

Readers who hold companies in the sector might consider switching to HPE stock after the earnings report. The non-GAAP P/E, for example, is 48.05% below the sector median (in the first line shown below).

Strong Backlog Driven By AI

Executives from other firms would mention “AI’ whenever they could to attract investor interest. Conversely, HPE reported artificial intelligence-related orders nearly doubled sequentially. Cumulative AI orders have risen steadily since Q1/2023.

AI revenue jumped in Q3, sharply above that of Q1 and Q2 levels. The $1.6 billion in revenue is now the highest.

Similarly, the company reported a record AI backlog in the third quarter.

Contribution from Juniper

HPE reported that annual recurring revenue exceeded $3 billion, which included Juniper. Revenue from networking grew, contributing to the outsized ARR growth. Software and services also spiked in Q3. This suggests that the company is cross-selling solutions to Juniper customers.

Readers may consider the following networking firms that I covered before. This includes Lumentum (LITE), Coherent (COHR), and Lumen Technologies (LUMN). The biggest player in this space is Cisco Systems (CSCO), which is worth considering.

On the conference call, Chief Executive Officer Antonio Neri said that Juniper will accelerate the company’s three strategic business pillars. They are networking, cloud, and AI. Integration of Juniper with HPE should result in a strong offering from both the HPE Intelligent Edge and HPE Networking segments.

The CEO said that AI growth benefits Juniper’s PTX routing business. Strong cybersecurity demand would drive demand for Aruba switches and Juniper firewalls.

Outlook

HPE offered a fourth-quarter outlook as shown below.

For the year, revenue will grow by up to 16%. Net earnings will contribute to free cash flow growth. Favorable working capital, helped by “significant improvements in accounts receivable collections,” will lift FCF. This suggests that HPE has customers with a healthy underlying business.

Opportunities

HPE expects server margins to trend back to historical levels of around 10% to 12%. It will manage product sales by offering pricing discounts differently (Gen11 and Gen12 compared to Gen10 or Gen10.5). As a result, expect margins in the 10% range in the fourth quarter.

The company faced a short-term impact from one deal that hurt server margins.

Juniper is a growth opportunity. The unit offers performance in data center switches, routing (automated WAN), and security. To illustrate the sales team’s effectiveness, the CEO walked through an employee onboarding process. The company offers the sales team incentives for selling both security and switching products.

The AI space is a strong opportunity. Juniper offers network products that customers need. The sovereign space is growing at over 200% Y/Y. HPE will tap this segment with its integrated rack-scale architecture.

In the enterprise space, demand for a fully integrated stack will support HPE’s revenue growth. It has the HPE ProLiant for the integrated infrastructure, Cray for the GPU market, and Alletra MP for storage. Investors may consider Seagate (STX), SanDisk (SNDK), and Western Digital (WDC) for pure exposure in the storage sector.

Cybersecurity is an ongoing opportunity. CEO Neri said that HPE has a robust security portfolio. It leads its offering with a secure network approach. That includes Juniper firewalls and secure access service edge (SSE). As SD-WAN and security converge, its 2020 acquisition of Silver Peak will continue to pay off.

Risks

AI is a growth opportunity across HPE’s three segments. Although spending by Meta Platforms (META), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOG) continues to rise, they may cut back on AI-related projects. That would slow Juniper network sales. HPE has a large customer base in 172 countries, so it is not reliant on a few customers. Still, if the AI momentum slows, companies would scrutinize the return on investment for expensive AI projects.

Your Takeaway

Hewlett-Packard Enterprise reported strong third-quarter results to justify the prior Buy rating. The company has rising business momentum. The Juniper acquisition will enable the firm to capture a wider addressable market in networking. Thanks to companies spending more on hardware to build AI-based solutions, HPE stock remains an attractive buy today.

data.title: Guggenheim Limited Duration Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

  • The Fund (Institutional Class) returned 1.51 percent, outperforming its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate 1-3 Years Index, by 0.24 percent.
  • Positive relative performance was driven by duration positioning and yield advantage over the benchmark.
  • Fixed-income spreads performed in line with the benchmark.

Strategy and Positioning

  • The Fund opportunistically increased credit exposure as spreads widened during the tariff-driven selloff, then tactically reduced most of this exposure as spreads tightened later in the quarter.
  • The Fund maintained a modest overweight duration stance.
  • As volatility declined near quarter end, the Fund continued to upgrade portfolio quality by selectively trimming high yield and BBB corporate exposure, particularly where spreads had retraced the most.
  • The Fund added to non-Agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) sector as the relative valuation has improved versus corporates and the sector benefits from stable underlying fundamentals.
  • The Fund increased exposure to front-sequential collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) and AAA-rated middle market collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) over the quarter.

QTD Contributors/Detractors

  • Carry contributed 1.3 percent to absolute return and 0.15 percent to relative return.
  • Duration contributed 0.1 percent on an absolute basis and was relatively flat on a relative basis.
  • Spread tightening in high yield corporates contributed marginally to performance.
  • Spread widening in asset-backed securities (ABS) and non-Agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) was a small relative detractor.

Economic and Market Review

  • The economic landscape continues to be shaped by heightened policy volatility, with historic swings in tariff policy the main source of uncertainty. Despite declining from its peak, the effective tariff rate remains at its highest level in decades at about 15 percent, acting as a tax on the economy.
  • While the full impact of tariff hikes has yet to materialize, the coming months are expected to bring slow growth and rising inflation as businesses absorb some of the higher tariff costs through lower profit margins and pass the rest on to consumers.
  • While peak uncertainty is likely behind us, there is likely to be continued caution from businesses and consumers. Downside risks may be tempered by the administration’s reluctance to maintain policies that could inflict significant economic harm.
  • Meanwhile, the federal budget, shaped by the One Big Beautiful Bill, should provide a modest boost to growth, primarily in 2026. We expect both consumer and business spending should get a lift from tax cuts early next year.
  • Although the bill contains more cost cutting measures than had been anticipated, concerns remain about fiscal sustainability. Even with a potential boost from tariff revenues, fiscal deficits would exceed 6 percent of gross domestic product (GDP) over the next decade. We see
  • U.S. real GDP growth of slightly below 1 percent in 2025, with a modest recovery in 2026.
  • In the near term, households are likely to experience reduced spending power due to higher prices on imported goods. Indeed, discretionary consumer spending has already seen a sharp pullback in the first half of the year. Business investment is also likely to slow, though AI-related investment remains a notable exception.
  • Weaker consumer and business spending will likely lead to more labor market softening, with unemployment rising to around 4.5 percent by year end.
  • We see inflation rising above 3 percent by the fourth quarter, driven by tariff-related price increases. However, this is expected to be a one-time boost rather than a persistent trend. Underlying disinflationary forces—such as cooling housing and wage inflation—should eventually prevail as higher unemployment and reduced consumer spending power help contain broader price pressures.
  • The Fed’s cautious stance reflects a wait-and-see approach to evolving policy and economic conditions. The Fed may have already been lowering the policy rate again had the uncertainty from tariff policy not been introduced.
  • As some time has now passed, we expect easing to commence later this year as policy certainty increases and labor market weakness becomes more pronounced, with rate cuts accelerating in 2026 to 3.125 percent as the Fed gains comfort that tariff driven price increases will not be an ongoing source of inflation. The Fed is expected to view risks of labor market weakness as a more persistent problem than tariff driven inflation.

Performance displayed represents past performance, which is no guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that when shares are redeemed, they may be worth more or less than original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For up-to-date fund performance, including performance current to the most recent month end, please visit our website at Guggenheim Investments. Load performance reflects maximum sales charges or contingent deferred sales charges (CDSC) as applicable. A Class has a maximum sales charge of 2.25%.

The referenced fund is offered in multiple share classes. Please read the prospectus for information on fees, expenses and holding periods that may apply to each class.

  1. Inception date of benchmark return is that of the fund’s oldest share class.
  2. The Net Expense Ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver by the Adviser through February 1, 2026, to limit the ordinary operating expenses of the fund.
  3. The Adjusted Expense Ratio (“Adjusted”) is based on the Net Expense Ratio, but excludes certain investment expenses, such as interest expense from borrowings and repurchase agreements and dividend expense from investments on short sales.
  4. Benchmark-Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond 1-3 Year Total Return Index.

Unless otherwise noted, data is as of 6.30.2025. Data subject to change. Partial year returns are cumulative. Returns reflect the reinvestment of dividends. The referenced index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment. Index performance does not reflect transaction costs, fees, or expenses. Index data source: FundStation.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Carry: The difference between the cost of financing an asset and the interest received on that asset. Dry powder: highly liquid assets, such as cash or money market instruments, that can be invested when more attractive investment opportunities arise. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors. In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities. § Investors in asset-backed securities, including collateralized loan obligations (“CLOs”), generally receive payments that are part interest and part return of principal. These payments may vary based on the rate loans are repaid. Some asset-backed securities may have structures that make their reaction to interest rates and other factors difficult to predict, making their prices volatile and they are subject to liquidity and valuation risk. § High yield and unrated debt securities are at a greater risk of default than investment grade bonds and may be less liquid, which may increase volatility. § When market conditions are deemed appropriate, the fund will leverage to the full extent permitted by its investment policies and restrictions and applicable law. Leveraging will exaggerate the effect on net asset value of any increase or decrease in the market value of the fund’s portfolio. § The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment. § Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments. § Investments in reverse repurchase agreements expose the fund to the many of the same risks as investments in derivatives. The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle. § The fund’s investments in foreign securities carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political, or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks). § Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks. § The fund’s investments in municipal securities can be affected by events that affect the municipal bond market. § The fund’s investments in real estate securities subject the fund to the same risks as direct investments in real estate, which is particularly sensitive to economic downturns. § The fund’s investments in restricted securities may involve financial and liquidity risk. § You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares. § It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government. Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Index Definition. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond 1–3 Year Total Return Index measures the performance of publicly issued investment grade corporate, U.S. Treasury and government agency securities with remaining maturities of one to three years.

This material is not intended as a recommendation or as investment advice of any kind, including in connection with rollovers, transfers, and distributions. Such material is not provided in a fiduciary capacity, may not be relied upon for or in connection with the making of investment decisions, and does not constitute a solicitation of an offer to buy or sell securities. All content has been provided for informational or educational purposes only and is not intended to be and should not be construed as legal or tax advice and/or a legal opinion. Always consult a financial, tax, and/or legal professional regarding your specific situation.

Read the fund’s prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) carefully before investing. It contains the fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges, expenses, and other information, which should be considered carefully before investing. Obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus (if available) at Guggenheim Investments.

The referenced fund is distributed by Guggenheim Funds Distributors, LLC. Guggenheim Investments represents the investment management businesses of Guggenheim Partners, LLC (“Guggenheim”), which includes Guggenheim Partners Investment Management (“GPIM”), the investment advisor to the referenced fund. Guggenheim Funds Distributors, LLC, is affiliated with Guggenheim and GPIM.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

data.title: Metro: Some Profit Lift From DC Investments Last Year
data.description:
Metro Inc. (TSX:MRU:CA) had a decent revenue performance, driven almost entirely by pricing and not volumes, particularly in food. Downtrading continues, and the "buying Canadian" product trend continues after the tariff spat. There is some pressure from vendors to institute price increases on their products, and that's started to intensify now, along with a general feeling that there is a deceleration in Canadian consumption activity.

In terms of the facilities that were coming online and that we mentioned in our previous coverage, most of the frictions have been lapped. While there are still some scale effects being hoped for, we have by now seen most of the extent of the efficiency benefits that should have come from those investments. CAPEX is at least on the way down, and that seems to be putting FCFY at a level competitive with peers like Tesco (OTCPK:TSCDF). Ultimately the performance hasn't been bad, and the business model is mostly resilient, with downtrading moving demand into their private label products at least.

Results

The performance wasn't bad. For the food business, sales were up 1.9%, with online food sales growing in the mix by growing 14.4%. Since the model is to work with third-party delivery apps, we understand that there is a negative mix effect that comes from this mix effect. For the pharmacy business, sales were up 5.5% YoY, with a pretty similar contribution between prescription sales at 6.2% in growth and 4% in front store sales. Gross margin climbed despite some negative mix effects and limited volume growth. The growth in the pharmacy business in the quarterly mix should be a positive mix effect. This comes down to mainly improvements in the distribution and improved retail efficiency as well. The margin went up 0.2%. Apparently the effects of the completed facilities would have been more noticeable absent inflation.

But the facilities have a bigger bearing on the SG&A. SG&A as a proportion of sales is similar to last quarter, and the benefits of the frictional and duplicate costs getting lapped have already been observed in these results. There is still some scale benefit being expected from the Fresh Phase 2 DC in Toronto related to maximizing automation. But other than that, we don't expect considerable structural margin improvements from this point onwards, with the new footprint also contributing now in higher depreciation costs. EBITDA was up 5.7% overall, a little ahead of overall sales growth.

For the food segment, the primary comments would be that there has been a shift to Canadian products. There is growth there, but overall concerns around the health of the Canadian consumer remain. Downtrading isn't getting more severe, but it is present at the moment with private label sales continuing to outperform national brands. CPI growth was over 3% against around a 2% growth in food sales, reflecting the negative volume effect against a more positive pricing effect. Promotionality is also on the up, and there is more supply of stores in the markets as well, though management hasn't noticed a considerable market effect from that. Discount and private label have both been the stronger areas.

The tariffs between the U.S. and Canada, particularly the counter tariffs, are affecting the market now, where vendors are pushing for price increases at Metro stores, which the company is trying to resist as much as possible to prevent demand destruction. Some stronger CPG groups are putting price increases in on their side already. As we understood it, the increases being requested are in the high single digits. It's not really clear that the Canadian consumer is going to be able to handle something like that this time.

The pharmacy business did well. Prescription sales were up 6.2%, driven by organic growth, specialty medications, and clinical services. This is growing in the mix, and it is a higher margin business.

Bottom Line

The facility investments seem to be mainly keeping margins from falling in a more difficult environment. More important than margin considerations now are actually just the lower investment intensities that should follow this previous period of investments. The CAPEX has been considerably falling since the end of these investments. The forward FCFY might be something like 6% on a run-rate basis when looking at Q3 run rates for investment spending and annualizing the operating cash flows.

Tesco is something like a 6% FCFY as well, based on latest FY data. We probably slightly prefer the Metro pharmacy tilt that Tesco doesn't really match. At the same FCFY, one could reason that one should prefer Metro. The reality is that neither business has an FCFY to really write home about. While we aren't too concerned about mounting pressures, there does seem to be some conflict with suppliers over price increases related to tariffs. We also see limited margin gain from the investments given some negative vectors. With promotionality on the rise, we don't expect a lot more new revenue growth from here. With our worsening outlook for the U.S., we would expect Canada to also head in a similar direction, as their economic fortunes are ultimately still quite linked.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Investing In Platinum And Palladium After The Correction With The SPPP ETF Product
data.description:
The two leading platinum group metals, platinum and palladium, had lagged their precious cousins, gold and silver, for years. Palladium reached a record high of $3,3425 per ounce in early 2022 before plunging below the $1,000 level. Platinum’s record high was in 2008 at $2308.80 per ounce. Platinum spent nearly a decade, from 2015 through Q1 2025, trading around a $1,000 per ounce pivot point.

Platinum and palladium broke out to the upside in Q2 2025, reversing the trends from the previous years. The last time I covered the Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust (NYSEARCA:SPPP) on Seeking Alpha was on October 16, 2024, when I concluded:

Adding SPPP to portfolios is a value approach to precious metals that could pay off over the coming months and years if the currency consolidation gives way to bullish trends in the thinly traded precious metals.

Nearby NYMEX platinum futures were trading at $1,007.80 per ounce on October 16, 2024, while the nearby NYMEX palladium futures were at $1,019 per ounce. The SPPP ETF was at the $10.04 per share level. Prices of the metals and SPPP have moved higher since October 2024.

Technical breakouts in Platinum and Palladium in Q2 2025

Platinum reached a record high of $2,308.80 in 2008. Since 2015, platinum traded around a $1,000 per ounce pivot point until Q2 2025.

The quarterly chart highlights the NYMEX platinum futures $909.40 to $1,063.80 trading range over the first three months of 2025. In Q2, the range was from $878.30 to $1,440.50 per ounce, with platinum settling Q2 at $1,343 on June 30, 2025. Platinum fell below the Q1 low in Q2 and settled above the high from Q1, forming a bullish key reversal pattern on the long-term chart. While platinum was trading around the $1,380 level in early September, the price rose to a high of $1,511.40 in Q3, the highest price since 2014.

The quarterly chart highlights the NYMEX palladium futures trading range of $901.00 to $1,077.50 over the first quarter of 2025. In Q2, the range was from $870.50 to $1,193.50 per ounce, with palladium settling Q2 at $1,107.10 on June 30, 2025. Like platinum, palladium fell below the Q1 low in Q2 and settled above the high from Q1, forming a bullish key reversal pattern on the long-term chart. While palladium was trading around the $1,145 level in early September, the price rose to a higher $1,373.50 high in Q3, which was the highest price since Q2 2023.

The bullish case for the rare platinum group metals

Platinum and palladium futures broke out to the upside in Q2 2025. The factors supporting higher prices include:

  • Platinum and palladium are rare precious metals, with annual mine supplies of 180 and 190 metric tons, respectively.
  • Russia and South Africa produce 75% of the precious metals, with South Africa leading in platinum output and Russia slightly edging out South Africa in palladium production.
  • The density and high melting points of the platinum group metals make them indispensable for a wide range of industrial applications.
  • Compared to gold and silver futures markets, platinum and palladium are far less liquid.

As of September 3, 2025:

  • COMEX gold futures open interest was 494,776 contracts. At $3,615 per ounce, the gold futures market value was above $178.86 billion.
  • COMEX silver futures open interest was 158,277 contracts. At $41.50 per ounce, the silver futures market value was above $32.84 billion.
  • The NYMEX platinum futures open interest stood at 89,968 contracts. At $1,380 per ounce, the platinum futures market value was above $6.20 billion.
  • The NYMEX palladium futures open interest stood at 18,687 contracts. At $1,145 per ounce, the palladium futures market value was just below $2.14 billion.

The bottom line is that less liquid markets often exhibit higher price variance, as bids to buy can disappear during selloffs and offers to sell may evaporate during rallies. Lower liquidity tends to result in significant price fluctuations. After putting in bullish key reversals on the long-term quarterly charts in Q2 2025, platinum and palladium could be in a position to experience further upside price action.

Gold and silver support higher prices

Gold and silver are in long-term bullish trends. Gold has been rising, making higher lows and higher highs since the 1999 bottom of $252.50 per ounce. Over the past eight consecutive quarters, the price of gold has increased to a new record high and was trading above the $3,600 per ounce level on September 4.

Silver’s path of least resistance has been bullish since the 2020 pandemic-inspired low of $11.64 per ounce. At over $41.40 per ounce in early September 2025, silver’s price is more than three and a half times higher than the 2020 low.

As market participants have turned to the security of precious metals over the past years, gold and silver have shone. With platinum over $900 per ounce below its all-time high from 2008 and palladium nearly $2,300 under its high from 2022, the platinum group metals trading on the CME’s NYMEX division offer significant value compared with gold and silver trading on the CME’s COMEX division. The bottom line is that the bullish price action in gold and silver, combined with reduced liquidity in platinum and palladium futures, could lead to parabolic rallies in the PGMs.

SPPP owns physical platinum and palladium

The fund summary for the Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust states:

As of September 3, 2025, SPPP held 168,124 ounces of platinum and 137,036 ounces of palladium. At $12.14 per share, SPPP had nearly $376 million in assets under management. SPPP trades an average of over 354,000 shares daily, making it a highly liquid ETF product. SPPP’s expense ratio is 1.05%.

No Seeking Alpha ETF Grades, but a bullish trend

Seeking Alpha does not provide ETF Grades for the SPPP ETF. However, the ETF that owns physical platinum and palladium has tracked the leading PGM prices in 2025.

Since the end of 2024:

  • Platinum at $1,380 per ounce was 51.6% higher than the 2024 closing price of $910.50.
  • Palladium at $1,145 per ounce was 25.9% higher than the 2024 closing price of $909.80.

The monthly chart highlights SPPP’s 36.6% rise from $8.89 at the end of last year to $12.14 on September 4, 2025, which reflects the average increases in platinum and palladium prices over the past eight months. SPPP reached a peak of $13.34 in July 2025, when platinum and palladium prices hit their respective highs.

Platinum and palladium prices have corrected from the July 2025 highs, which could be the perfect time to consider adding the metals to portfolios. Industrial and precious metals offer significant value compared to gold and silver in the current market environment. Moreover, a lack of liquidity in the PGM futures could lead to a parabolic rally over the coming months. The SPPP ETF owns physical platinum and palladium bullion and does an excellent job tracking the metals’ prices.
